source: Arka astronomy index (bright stars, planets, and notable objects; public astronomical references)

objects.name: Betelgeuse
objects.description: A red supergiant marking Orion's shoulder. It is one of the largest known stars and varies in brightness. It will end its life as a supernova, though timing is uncertain.

objects.name: Sirius
objects.description: The brightest star in Earth's night sky. Sirius A is a main-sequence star; Sirius B is a white dwarf companion.

objects.name: Vega
objects.description: A bright northern star used historically for photometric calibration. It rotates rapidly and has an infrared excess from a debris disk.

objects.name: Polaris
objects.description: The current north celestial pole star, useful for navigation in the Northern Hemisphere. It is a multi-star system with a Cepheid primary.

objects.name: Rigel
objects.description: Orion's brightest star, a luminous blue supergiant at the hunter's foot. It outshines Betelgeuse despite appearing second in the constellation.

objects.name: Aldebaran
objects.description: The eye of Taurus the bull, an orange giant star. It lies along the line of sight to the Hyades cluster but is not a member.

objects.name: Proxima Centauri
objects.description: The closest known star to the Sun. It hosts at least two confirmed exoplanets and is a flare-active red dwarf.

objects.name: Sun
objects.description: The star at the center of the Solar System. Age ~4.6 billion years; supports life on Earth via photosynthesis and climate.

objects.name: Moon
objects.description: Earth's only natural satellite. Synodic period ~29.5 days; drives tides and stabilizes Earth's axial tilt.

objects.name: Mercury
objects.description: Innermost planet; fast 88-day orbit. Best seen at dawn or dusk near greatest elongation. No atmosphere; extreme temperature swings.

objects.name: Venus
objects.description: Brightest planet in the sky. Thick CO₂ atmosphere with sulfuric acid clouds; runaway greenhouse effect. Phases visible in a telescope.

objects.name: Mars
objects.description: The Red Planet with iron-oxide dust, polar ice caps, and the largest volcano (Olympus Mons) in the Solar System.

objects.name: Jupiter
objects.description: Largest planet; Great Red Spot storm; four large Galilean moons visible in binoculars. Dominates the outer Solar System gravitationally.

objects.name: Saturn
objects.description: Famous ringed planet. Rings are ice and rock particles. Titan is its largest moon with a thick atmosphere.

objects.name: Uranus
objects.description: Ice giant with extreme axial tilt (~98°). Faint naked-eye object under dark skies; best in binoculars or a telescope.

objects.name: Neptune
objects.description: Outermost major planet; deep blue from methane absorption. Requires binoculars or a telescope. Strongest winds in the Solar System.

objects.name: Orion
objects.description: Prominent winter constellation in the Northern Hemisphere. Contains Betelgeuse, Rigel, and the Orion Nebula (M42).

objects.name: Andromeda Galaxy
objects.description: Nearest major galaxy to the Milky Way. Visible as a faint smudge to the naked eye under dark skies; on a collision course with our galaxy.

objects.name: Pleiades
objects.description: Young open cluster easily seen with the naked eye. Blue reflection nebulosity surrounds hot stars; prominent in winter skies.

objects.name: International Space Station
objects.description: Crewed orbital laboratory at ~400 km altitude. Bright passes visible shortly after dusk or before dawn. Use 'arka astronomy iss' for pass times.
